commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: A maintainer/security owner should decide the probe shape and host-policy guidance before this draft PR moves toward merge.; Needs attention: The patch is security-sensitive because it recommends host namespace policy repairs for a sandbox failure path.; Review finding: [P2] Match the bwrap probe to the egress mode — src/commands/doctor-sandbox.ts:90 (sha=9fa4f8e7b7c623b878da069b4310ee38dbe6cf2b)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a Linux doctor probe for Codex bwrap namespace failures, Ubuntu/AppArmor repair guidance in docs, a changelog entry, and focused doctor coverage.

Reproducibility: yes. source and linked live reports give a high-confidence path for the Codex bwrap namespace failure on Ubuntu/AppArmor hosts. I did not rerun the live host repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: A maintainer/security owner should decide the probe shape and host-policy guidance before this draft PR moves toward merge.

target.title: fix(doctor): detect Codex bwrap namespace denials
target.body:
## Summary

- add a Linux doctor probe for the Codex bwrap namespace primitive used by `workspace-write`
- warn operators when host policy blocks unprivileged user/network namespaces under Docker sandbox mode
- document the Ubuntu/AppArmor repair path without adding another OpenClaw config option
- add a changelog entry and focused doctor coverage

## Root cause

The failing path is not an OpenClaw sandbox boundary selection problem. On Ubuntu/AppArmor hosts, the OpenClaw service user can be denied the user namespace and network namespace setup that Codex's bundled bwrap needs before shell startup. That shows up as `bwrap: setting up uid map: Permission denied` or `bwrap: loopback: Failed RTM_NEWADDR: Operation not permitted`.

This patch keeps the app-server policy unchanged and points `openclaw doctor` at the actual host primitive instead.

## Verification

- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs CHANGELOG.md docs/gateway/sandboxing.md docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-sandbox.ts src/commands/doctor-sandbox.warns-sandbox-enabled-without-docker.test.ts`
- live Ubuntu/AppArmor repro: unprivileged bwrap/unshare fails with uid-map and loopback namespace denials; kernel logs show AppArmor `unprivileged_userns` denials
- live Debian comparison: same unprivileged bwrap/unshare primitive succeeds
- Testbox `tbx_01krxyz336skxvd2tpywkk6zha`, Actions https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26046624448: `pnpm check:changed` completed, focused doctor test passed in the changed test lane, then `pnpm test:changed` failed on unchanged `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenClaw now diagnoses the host namespace policy failure that blocks Codex bwrap before shell startup on Ubuntu/AppArmor sandboxed gateway deployments.

Real environment tested: Ubuntu 24.04.4 and Debian 13 live OpenClaw instances, plus Blacksmith Testbox for the changed doctor/check lane.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw doctor` now runs `unshare --user --map-root-user --net true` when Docker sandbox mode is enabled and Docker is available.

Evidence after fix: the new doctor path emits a Sandbox warning containing the bwrap symptoms, the captured probe result, and host-policy repair guidance instead of suggesting another OpenClaw config boundary.

Observed result after fix: the code and docs direct the operator to repair Ubuntu/AppArmor namespace policy for the OpenClaw service user, commonly via `kernel.apparmor_restrict_unprivileged_userns=0` or an equivalent AppArmor profile, and explicitly avoid broad Docker privilege escalation.

What was not tested: a full green `pnpm test:changed` for this rebased commit; the prior Testbox run was blocked by an unrelated unchanged agent reliability test, and a final local focused vitest attempt timed out in the linked worktree before assertion output.

Refs #83018.
target.author: vincentkoc
